title: Instructions for Repairing a Robot Black Boy
description:
[1. TROUBLESHOOTING] Recently, I’ve been distracted by the fact that all the Black men in my family are gone. I’m the last one. And these dead guys won’t leave me alone. Every essay, poem, and cryptic Facebook update for the past five months has veered into my obsession with them.  What a joke it is […]

The post Instructions for Repairing a Robot Black Boy appeared first on
hook: A Black poet confronts the men in his family who haunt him—dead but refusing to leave his essays, his poems, his life.

title: ‘The Worst Neighbor Ever’
description: "He moved to the block promising a new bookstore. He brought a whole lot more than that. Now no one is quite sure how to describe what happened outside Quirky Books."
hook: A man promised a quirky bookstore to a Brooklyn block. What actually happened is far stranger and more unsettling than anyone expected.

title: In the Ring with Frederick Wiseman
description: “ ‘Is Frederick Wiseman, like, Frederick Wiseman?’ I asked Richard. Frederick Wiseman was indeed Frederick Wiseman: in his late seventies and shooting his thirty-eighth film.”
hook: Inside a conversation with Frederick Wiseman at 78, shooting his 38th film—a portrait of a master documentarian still asking radical questions.

title: Preston Sturges: Hail the Monkeying Hero
description: No artist in movies has ever made me feel better about being an American than Preston Sturges, and since the shame of being American is the specific ache lengthening my nights these days, I’ve found myself applying his movies medicinally, and with new gratitude.
hook: Preston Sturges movies are this critic's medicine for American shame—slapstick as resistance, comedy as the only honest response.

title: Kink in the Archive: The pleasures of porn in public
description: Helena Whittingham and Harlan Whittingham's careful curation enthusiastically invites audiences to consider porn as artefact and art.
hook: A thoughtful curation invites you to see pornography in a museum context—as material history, as art, as archive.
